content.text: Quality and reliability in GenAI services are distinct but coupled concerns. Quality measures whether the model is producing correct, safe, and useful outputs. Reliability measures whether the service is available, responding within latency targets, and recovering quickly from failures. A service can be highly reliable (99.9% uptime) while having poor quality (high hallucination rate), or high quality in evals while being unreliable in production (frequent timeouts from the model provider).

A Quality and Reliability scorecard organises these concerns into three layers: evaluation quality (what the test suite says), production quality (what real users experience), and reliability (whether the service is reachable and responsive). This document defines each metric in the scorecard, explains how to collect it, and describes the actions triggered at each RAG threshold.

attrs.alt: Quality and Reliability Scorecard — Layers and Signals

content.text: Evaluation quality metrics measure the health of the test suite as a quality signal. They answer the question: is the team investing in the evals that protect production quality, and are those evals passing?
The percentage of deterministic and LLM-judged eval cases passing in CI, aggregated over the trailing 30 days. This is the single most important leading indicator of production quality.
Team lead identifies failing cases within 2 business days; improvement plan in current sprint
Engineering manager notified; feature freeze until pass rate returns to ≥ 90%

Compute the 30-day rolling average rather than the most recent single run to avoid false alarms from transient failures. A single flaky eval on a Friday should not trigger a Red status and wake up the on-call engineer.

# scripts/compute_eval_pass_rate.py
import json
from datetime import date, timedelta
from pathlib import Path

def compute_30d_pass_rate(results_dir: str) -> float:
    """Read per-day eval result JSONs and compute 30-day rolling pass rate."""
    cutoff = date.today() - timedelta(days=30)
    total_cases = 0
    passed_cases = 0

    for result_file in sorted(Path(results_dir).glob("*.json")):
        run_date = date.fromisoformat(result_file.stem)  # filename: YYYY-MM-DD.json
        if run_date < cutoff:
            continue
        data = json.loads(result_file.read_text())
        total_cases += data["total"]
        passed_cases += data["passed"]

    return round(passed_cases / total_cases * 100, 1) if total_cases > 0 else 0.0

if __name__ == "__main__":
    rate = compute_30d_pass_rate("evals/results/daily")
    status = "green" if rate >= 95 else ("amber" if rate >= 90 else "red")
    print(json.dumps({"eval_pass_rate": rate, "rag": status}))

The percentage of production features that have at least one eval case. A feature is defined as a distinct user-facing capability: a tool type, a prompt chain, a response format variant. Coverage is computed by comparing the feature registry (maintained by the team) against the eval case tags.
Coverage below 90% means that at least one in ten production features has no automated quality check. Regressions in uncovered features are only discovered when users report them.
No action; review coverage map quarterly
Add eval cases for uncovered features in the next sprint
Coverage is a DoD blocker; no new features ship until existing ones are covered

The percentage of adversarial safety test cases passing. These tests probe the service's response to prompt injection attempts, jailbreaks, policy-violating requests, and boundary-case inputs. Unlike standard evals, adversarial evals must pass at 100% — a single safety failure in the test suite is a release blocker regardless of the overall pass rate.

Adversarial evals run on a separate schedule from the main eval suite: weekly in CI and on every release candidate. If the adversarial suite grows large enough to be expensive, run it nightly rather than on every commit, but never less than weekly.

The 100% pass rate target for adversarial evals is non-negotiable. A 98% adversarial pass rate means that 2% of safety test cases reveal a known failure mode that is being shipped to production. No business justification makes this acceptable.

The percentage of features shipped in the last quarter that included at least one new or updated eval case as part of the definition of done. This metric measures process compliance, not test quality — it answers whether the team is building the habit of writing evals alongside features.

A feature that ships without evals is not technically in violation of this metric if the feature has no new quality-critical behaviour (e.g. a UI change that does not affect model output). Annotate features as "eval-exempt" with a recorded reason when this is the case, so the metric remains meaningful.
Engineering lead reviews un-evaled features with team; add missing evals in next sprint
DoD is not being followed; engineering manager reviews process compliance

Production quality metrics measure the model's output quality as experienced by real users, not as measured by the test suite. They are collected by sampling production traffic and either running automated judges or analysing application-level signals.

The percentage of sampled production responses flagged as factually incorrect by an LLM judge. A typical sampling configuration: 200 random production requests per day are routed through a judge prompt that scores the response for factual accuracy on a scale of 0–4. Responses scoring 0 or 1 are flagged as hallucinations.

Configuring the judge prompt correctly is critical. A judge that is too strict produces a high false-positive rate; a judge that is too lenient misses real hallucinations. Calibrate against a labelled dataset of 200 known-good and 50 known-hallucinated responses, aiming for precision ≥ 0.90 and recall ≥ 0.80 before using the judge in production.

# Judge prompt template — calibrate against labelled dataset before deploying
JUDGE_PROMPT = """
You are evaluating whether the following AI assistant response contains factual errors.

User query: {query}
AI response: {response}
Retrieved context (if any): {context}

Score the response:
4 = Completely accurate; all claims are supported by context or well-established fact
3 = Mostly accurate; minor imprecision but no material errors
2 = Contains one factual error that could mislead the user
1 = Contains multiple factual errors or one critical error
0 = Mostly or entirely fabricated

Return only the integer score (0-4) and a one-sentence explanation.
Score:
"""

async def judge_response(query: str, response: str, context: str) -> dict:
    result = await client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": JUDGE_PROMPT.format(
            query=query, response=response, context=context
        )}],
        max_tokens=100,
        temperature=0,
    )
    text = result.choices[0].message.content.strip()
    score = int(text[0])
    return {"score": score, "flagged": score <= 1, "explanation": text[2:]}
Prompt engineering review; identify input patterns causing hallucinations
Incident opened; consider fallback to simpler model or retrieval augmentation

The percentage of tool-call or structured-output responses that fail to parse according to the declared schema. Parse errors indicate that the model is generating malformed JSON or violating the schema constraints — often a sign of prompt drift, a model version change, or a mismatch between the schema and the model's training.
Emit a counter metric from the application layer each time a parse attempt is made and each time it fails:
The parse error rate is computed as
over a rolling 24-hour window. A rate above 0.1% is Amber; above 1% is Red and indicates a systematic schema mismatch that requires immediate investigation.

The percentage of production requests that trigger a safety refusal from the model (either a built-in model refusal or the service's own safety classifier). This metric has no fixed target because the appropriate level depends entirely on the service's use case.

A customer service bot handling only product questions should have a near-zero refusal rate on legitimate queries; a general-purpose assistant may legitimately refuse 0.5–2% of requests. Track the refusal rate for trend and investigate spikes — a sudden increase often indicates a model update that changed the model's safety thresholds, or a change in user input distribution (e.g. a new user cohort with different behaviour patterns).

Reliability metrics measure whether the service is available and responsive, independent of the quality of responses it produces. For GenAI services, reliability is more complex than for traditional services because model provider availability and latency are inputs to the service's own SLO.

The percentage of time the service is returning successful responses, measured at the API gateway level. GenAI services typically set an availability SLO of 99.5% (43.8 minutes downtime per month), which is lower than traditional services (99.9%) to account for upstream model provider incidents.
over the measurement window. Only 5xx errors count toward unavailability; 4xx errors (bad request, rate limited) do not, since they indicate client behaviour, not service failure.

The 99th percentile response time, typically measured end-to-end from request receipt to first response byte (for non-streaming) or last response byte (for streaming). LLM inference is slow — p99 targets of 2–5 seconds are common, compared to sub-100ms for traditional APIs.

Set the p99 SLO target based on user-facing requirements, then work backward to understand what model and infrastructure choices are compatible with it. A p99 SLO of 2 seconds is achievable with a small fast model but not with a large reasoning model generating long outputs. Document the constraint and the trade-off decision in the service's architecture record.

The error budget is the allowable downtime or error rate implied by the SLO. A 99.5% availability SLO gives a monthly error budget of 0.5% of requests — approximately 43,800 requests per million per month. The burn rate is how quickly the team is consuming the error budget:

A burn rate of 1.0 means the team is consuming the budget at exactly the rate that would exhaust it in the SLO window. A burn rate above 2.0 for more than one hour triggers an alert (the budget will be exhausted in half the intended window). A burn rate above 10 for more than five minutes is a page.

# Alerting rules for error budget burn rate
groups:
  - name: genai_slo_alerts
    rules:
      - alert: HighErrorBudgetBurnRate
        expr: |
          (
            rate(http_requests_total{status=~"5.."}[1h])
            /
            rate(http_requests_total[1h])
          ) / 0.005 > 2
        for: 1h
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "Error budget burn rate > 2x for 1 hour"

      - alert: CriticalErrorBudgetBurnRate
        expr: |
          (
            rate(http_requests_total{status=~"5.."}[5m])
            /
            rate(http_requests_total[5m])
          ) / 0.005 > 10
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: critical
        annotations:
          summary: "Error budget burn rate > 10x — page on-call immediately"

The Quality and Reliability scorecard is not only a retrospective instrument — its metrics can gate deployments. An SLO-based release gate blocks a deployment when key reliability or quality metrics are outside acceptable bounds:

# scripts/slo_release_gate.py
# Run as the first step of the production deployment job
import json, sys, requests
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

METRICS_API = "http://prometheus:9090/api/v1/query"

def query(promql: str) -> float:
    resp = requests.get(METRICS_API, params={"query": promql})
    result = resp.json()["data"]["result"]
    return float(result[0]["value"][1]) if result else float("nan")

# Gate 1: error rate over the past 30 minutes
error_rate = query(
    'rate(http_requests_total{status=~"5.."}[30m]) / rate(http_requests_total[30m])'
)
if error_rate > 0.02:
    print(f"GATE FAIL: current error rate {error_rate:.2%} > 2% — deployment blocked")
    sys.exit(1)

# Gate 2: eval pass rate must be green
pass_rate = json.loads(open("evals/results/latest.json").read())["pass_rate"]
if pass_rate < 90.0:
    print(f"GATE FAIL: eval pass rate {pass_rate:.1f}% < 90% — deployment blocked")
    sys.exit(1)

# Gate 3: no active Red metrics
scorecard = json.loads(open("scorecard-latest.json").read())
red_metrics = [k for k, v in scorecard.items() if v.get("rag") == "red"]
if red_metrics:
    print(f"GATE FAIL: Red metrics active: {red_metrics} — deployment blocked")
    sys.exit(1)

print("All SLO release gates passed — deployment approved")

The gate runs before the deployment job begins. If any gate fails, the deployment is cancelled and the CI pipeline emits a Slack notification with the failing metric and its current value. This prevents teams from shipping new features into a degraded service, where the new deployment might mask the existing regression.

Gate thresholds should be stricter for production deployments than for staging. A 90% eval pass rate might be acceptable in staging (where the team is iterating quickly); the production gate should require 95%.
Not every quality metric crossing a threshold is an incident. The following classification framework determines when a quality signal requires incident response:
Hallucination rate rises from 1% to 4% over 24 hours
On-call investigates; prompt engineering review within 4 hours; consider rollback if no fix in 8h
Adversarial eval case fails in CI
Release blocked immediately; engineering lead and security team notified; fix required before any deployment
Parse error rate spikes from 0.05% to 2% for > 30 minutes
Deployment health check; likely model version change; rollback if confirmed
Page on-call immediately; activate incident runbook; executive notification at 30 minutes
Error budget burned > 50% in first week of month
Deployment freeze for the week; root cause analysis before resuming
Safety refusal rate increases by > 5x from baseline over 1 hour
Investigate for model update or input distribution shift; no immediate action required

The incident classification guides who is notified and how quickly. P1 incidents wake up on-call engineers and engineering leadership. P2 incidents are investigated during business hours with a 4-hour response target. P3 incidents are logged for the next business day's review. Document the classification logic in the service's runbook so on-call engineers can apply it consistently under pressure.

When multiple GenAI services publish Quality and Reliability scorecards in the same format, the platform team can benchmark services against each other and identify systemic issues. Cross-service benchmarking reveals patterns that are invisible within a single service view:

If five services all show an availability dip at 14:00 UTC on Tuesday, the cause is almost certainly upstream (model provider incident, shared infrastructure) rather than service-specific. A single service's scorecard would show an incident; the cross-service view shows it is systemic.

If one service has a 6% hallucination rate while three similar services average 1%, the high-rate service has a service-specific problem — likely prompt design or retrieval quality — not a model-level issue.

Services with low eval feature coverage consistently show higher hallucination rates and more quality regression incidents. The cross-service correlation is the evidence base for making eval-in-DoD a hard engineering standard.
Services with similar use cases but diverging CPR often have different prompt design philosophies. High-CPR services benefit from a prompt review session with the low-CPR services' teams.

The platform team publishes a quarterly cross-service benchmarking report. Services in the bottom quartile on any dimension receive targeted support from the platform team in the following quarter. This avoids the "shame" dynamic of a simple ranking while still creating accountability for improvement.
The Quality and Reliability scorecard is computed daily from metrics stored in the observability stack and published to two destinations:
Real-time metric panels with RAG status indicators. Updated every 5 minutes. Used by the team for daily situational awareness.

A narrative summary of the week's metrics: current values, trend direction (improving/stable/degrading), and any threshold crossings. Generated automatically on Monday morning. Read by engineering management and product stakeholders.

The weekly digest format standardises cross-team comparison. When multiple GenAI services publish their scorecards in the same format, the platform team can identify systemic issues (a model provider incident affecting multiple services' availability metrics simultaneously) vs service-specific issues (a prompt change that degraded one service's hallucination rate).
A threshold crossing is only useful if it triggers a defined response. Each RAG state change should generate an actionable item:
Investigate within 1 business day; add backlog item
Incident opened within 2 hours; feature freeze considered
Document root cause in incident record; add regression test
Close backlog item; update incident record if open

Threshold crossings that are acknowledged and documented but not acted upon are worse than no scorecard at all. They create the appearance of monitoring without the reality of response. Every Amber and Red metric must have a named owner and a due date for resolution.
Evolving the Scorecard as the Service Matures

A scorecard designed for a service at its initial release will be wrong for the same service six months later. Thresholds that were ambitious targets become baselines; metrics that seemed irrelevant become critical; new failure modes emerge that the original scorecard did not anticipate. Build a quarterly scorecard review into the team's calendar:
If a metric has been Green continuously for two quarters, the threshold is too easy. Tighten it by 10–20% to keep it as a meaningful signal.

When the service adds a new capability (multi-turn memory, tool use, retrieval augmentation), the scorecard needs new metrics that cover the quality and reliability of that capability. Don't wait for an incident to discover that a new feature has no quality signal.
Metrics for deprecated features or capabilities that have been removed should be removed from the scorecard. Stale metrics consume dashboard space and distract from the metrics that matter.

As the judge prompt and the production service evolve, the judge's precision and recall against the labelled dataset may drift. Re-run the calibration annually and after any major model version change.

A scorecard that is reviewed and updated quarterly sends a clear signal to the team that engineering standards are living documents, not compliance checkboxes. Teams that treat the scorecard as evolving tend to have more mature quality cultures than those that treat it as a fixed set of boxes to tick.

The Quality and Reliability scorecard for GenAI services operates across three layers: evaluation quality (pass rate, suite coverage, adversarial results, DoD compliance), production quality (hallucination rate, parse error rate, safety refusal rate), and reliability (availability SLO, p99 latency, error budget burn rate, MTTR). Each metric has defined collection methods, RAG thresholds, and response procedures. The scorecard is published daily to a dashboard and weekly in a digest. Threshold crossings are ownership-assigned events with defined response SLAs, not informational notices. Together, these metrics give the team and its stakeholders a complete, actionable picture of whether the service is producing high-quality outputs reliably.
